questions.question: If standard input is a terminal, redirect it so that terminal sessions do not mistakenly consider the terminal to be used by the command.
questions.explanation: If standard input is a terminal, redirect it so that terminal sessions do not mistakenly consider the terminal to be used by the command.

questions.question: If standard input at least from −20 (process has high priority and gets more resources, thus slowing down other processes) through 19 (process has lower priority and runs slowly itself, but has less impact on the speed of other running processes).
questions.explanation: If standard input is a terminal, redirect it so that terminal sessions do not mistakenly consider the terminal to be used by the command.

questions.question: Make the substitute file descriptor unreadable, so that commands that mistakenly attempt to read from standard input can report an error.
questions.explanation: Make the substitute file descriptor unreadable, so that commands that mistakenly attempt to read from standard input can report an error.

questions.question: Make the substitute at least from −20 (process has high priority and gets more resources, thus slowing down other processes) through 19 (process has lower priority and runs slowly itself, but has less impact on the speed of other running processes).
questions.explanation: Make the substitute file descriptor unreadable, so that commands that mistakenly attempt to read from standard input can report an error.

questions.question: This redirection is a GNU extension; programs intended to be portable to non-GNU hosts can use ‘ nohup command [ arg ]… 0>/dev/null ’ instead.
questions.explanation: This redirection is a GNU extension; programs intended to be portable to non-GNU hosts can use ‘ nohup command [ arg ]… 0>/dev/null ’ instead.

questions.question: This redirection is at least from −20 (process has high priority and gets more resources, thus slowing down other processes) through 19 (process has lower priority and runs slowly itself, but has less impact on the speed of other running processes).
questions.explanation: This redirection is a GNU extension; programs intended to be portable to non-GNU hosts can use ‘ nohup command [ arg ]… 0>/dev/null ’ instead.

questions.question: If standard output is a terminal, the command’s standard output is appended to the file nohup.out ; if that cannot be written to, it is appended to the file $HOME/nohup.out ; and if that cannot be written to, the command is not run.
questions.explanation: If standard output is a terminal, the command’s standard output is appended to the file nohup.out ; if that cannot be written to, it is appended to the file $HOME/nohup.out ; and if that cannot be written to, the command is not run.

questions.question: If standard output at least from −20 (process has high priority and gets more resources, thus slowing down other processes) through 19 (process has lower priority and runs slowly itself, but has less impact on the speed of other running processes).
questions.explanation: If standard output is a terminal, the command’s standard output is appended to the file nohup.out ; if that cannot be written to, it is appended to the file $HOME/nohup.out ; and if that cannot be written to, the command is not run.

questions.question: Any nohup.out or $HOME/nohup.out file created by nohup is made readable and writable only to the user, regardless of the current umask settings.
questions.explanation: Any nohup.out or $HOME/nohup.out file created by nohup is made readable and writable only to the user, regardless of the current umask settings.

questions.question: Any nohup.out or at least from −20 (process has high priority and gets more resources, thus slowing down other processes) through 19 (process has lower priority and runs slowly itself, but has less impact on the speed of other running processes).
questions.explanation: Any nohup.out or $HOME/nohup.out file created by nohup is made readable and writable only to the user, regardless of the current umask settings.

questions.question: If standard error is a terminal, it is normally redirected to the same file descriptor as the (possibly-redirected) standard output.
questions.explanation: If standard error is a terminal, it is normally redirected to the same file descriptor as the (possibly-redirected) standard output.

questions.question: If standard error at least from −20 (process has high priority and gets more resources, thus slowing down other processes) through 19 (process has lower priority and runs slowly itself, but has less impact on the speed of other running processes).
questions.explanation: If standard error is a terminal, it is normally redirected to the same file descriptor as the (possibly-redirected) standard output.

questions.question: However, if standard output is closed, standard error terminal output is instead appended to the file nohup.out or $HOME/nohup.out as above.
questions.explanation: However, if standard output is closed, standard error terminal output is instead appended to the file nohup.out or $HOME/nohup.out as above.

questions.question: nohup does not automatically put the command it runs in the background; you must do that explicitly, by ending the command line with an ‘ & ’.
questions.explanation: nohup does not automatically put the command it runs in the background; you must do that explicitly, by ending the command line with an ‘ & ’.

questions.question: nohup does not at least from −20 (process has high priority and gets more resources, thus slowing down other processes) through 19 (process has lower priority and runs slowly itself, but has less impact on the speed of other running processes).

questions.question: Also, nohup does not alter the niceness of command ; use nice for that, e.g., ‘ nohup nice command ’.
questions.explanation: Also, nohup does not alter the niceness of command ; use nice for that, e.g., ‘ nohup nice command ’.

questions.question: The `nproc invocation` --all option Print the number of installed processors on the system, which may be greater than the number online or available to the current process.
